target: SFOS 22.0.0.411 -- Validation of SFOS-016, SFOS-024, SFOS-025
description: Validation of three high-severity findings: tar path traversal, HA synccmd injection, and SSO missing-return auth bypass

validations.title: Tar Extraction Path Traversal via crimport
validations.validation_method: Dynamic -- crafted tar file with absolute path entries extracted via simulated apiparser_extract_tar.sh
validations.result: A tar file containing an entry with absolute path '/tmp/sfos_024_validated.txt' was successfully extracted to that absolute path, escaping the intended /sdisk/api-<timestamp>/ extraction directory. BusyBox tar (used on SFOS at /bin/tar) does NOT have GNU tar's '--absolute-names' protection or '..' member rejection. The script calls 'tar -xvf' with no safety flags.
validations.proof: File /tmp/sfos_024_validated.txt created with content 'SFOS-024 VALIDATED: Arbitrary file write via tar path traversal in apiparser_extract_tar.sh'. Attempt to write to /etc/cron.d/ was blocked only by test environment user permissions -- would succeed on SFOS running as root.
validations.exploitability: Requires XML API to be enabled (non-default) and attacker IP in the allowlist. On SFOS, api_parser runs with root privileges, so extracted files are owned by root. Writing to /etc/cron.d/, /etc/init.d/, or SSH authorized_keys achieves code execution.

validations.title: Command Injection via HA synccmd in Tar Extraction
validations.status: DOWNGRADED -- Not exploitable with current input sanitization
validations.validation_method: Code review of isSafeFileName() + shell expansion testing of synccmd string construction
validations.result: The synccmd string construction ('mkdir ${FOLDER};cd ${FOLDER};tar -xvf $TARFILE') IS inherently unsafe (string-based shell execution with variable interpolation). However, isSafeFileName() blocks all useful shell metacharacters: $ ` ; & | > < *. The FOLDER and LOGFILE variables are internally generated timestamps. TARFILE path is sanitized by isSafeFileName before reaching the script. No exploitable injection path exists with current sanitization.
validations.exploitability: Not exploitable in current code. Would become exploitable if: (1) isSafeFileName is bypassed, (2) a new code path sets TARFILE without sanitization, or (3) the regex is weakened.
validations.notes: Reclassified as defense-in-depth weakness. The synccmd design pattern should use array-based execution instead of string interpolation.

validations.title: SSOAdminServlet Missing Return After Auth Failure
validations.result: Bytecode-level analysis confirms two missing-return bugs: (1) After schedule restriction sendRedirect at bytecode offset 794, execution falls through to prepareSSOSessionBean at 806 with no goto/return. (2) After getUserID()==-1 sendRedirect at bytecode offset 839, execution falls through to getSession(true) at 846 and setAccessToken at 869. Both paths result in a valid HTTP session being created on the server despite the client receiving a redirect response.
validations.proof: javap output shows: bytecode 794: sendRedirect -> 806: prepareSSOSessionBean (no intervening goto/return). bytecode 839: sendRedirect -> 846: getSession(true) (no intervening goto/return). In Java, sendRedirect does NOT halt method execution -- it only sets the response header. Without an explicit return, all subsequent code executes.
validations.exploitability: An attacker who ignores the HTTP redirect retains a JSESSIONID cookie tied to a server-side session with: (a) sessionBean containing the access token, (b) sessionBean stored in the session attributes. The session's userID may be -1 (for non-existent user) or valid (for schedule-restricted user). Subsequent requests with this JSESSIONID would be processed as authenticated. The practical impact depends on whether userID=-1 passes downstream ACL checks.

validations.title: X-Forwarded-Email Header Trust -- Privilege Escalation
validations.status: CONFIRMED via source code and bytecode analysis -- live validation still needed for full exploitability assessment
validations.validation_method: Source code review (decompiled via jadx and CFR) + bytecode verification
validations.result: SSOAdminServlet line 51 reads X-Forwarded-Email directly from request.getHeader() and passes it to UserHelper.getRecordByEmail() for user lookup. The only guard is validateSSOCookie() at line 41 which checks the oauth2-proxy cookie against 127.0.0.1:65020/oauth2/auth. The X-Forwarded-Email header is NOT cross-validated against the authenticated SSO session. Code-level confirmation is definitive. The remaining unknown is whether oauth2-proxy overwrites the X-Forwarded-Email header for all proxied requests (which would prevent client injection).
validations.exploitability: If the webconsole Jetty port (4444 via Apache proxy) accepts direct connections without going through oauth2-proxy, an attacker with a valid SSO session can set X-Forwarded-Email to any admin's email. The standard Sophos deployment routes /webconsole/SSOAdminController through oauth2-proxy, but the architecture allows direct access to port 8009 (Jetty) from localhost. The exploitability depends on network architecture: (a) If only oauth2-proxy can reach Jetty on port 8009, exploitation requires oauth2-proxy to not overwrite client-injected headers. (b) If Apache on port 4444 also proxies to Jetty 8009 without stripping X-Forwarded-Email, direct exploitation is possible.
validations.notes: Live validation with a running SFOS + SSO configuration would definitively confirm whether the header can be injected by the client. The code-level evidence is unambiguous that the header is trusted without cross-validation.

summary: Three findings validated: SFOS-024 (tar path traversal) CONFIRMED at HIGH, SFOS-025 (synccmd injection) DOWNGRADED to LOW due to effective input sanitization, SFOS-015/016 (SSO missing-return and X-Forwarded-Email) CONFIRMED via bytecode analysis.
